name: Kel'Thuzad
aliases: founder of the Cult of the Damned

physicalDetails.detail: Not seen directly in chapter 8, but canon and story materials frame him as tall, gaunt, grey-haired, and scholar-thin: the body of a man shaped by lecture halls, research benches, and decades of neglecting ordinary flesh for more interesting work.
Even off-page, the chapter makes his old Kirin Tor identity part of his silhouette. He reads less like a warlord in armor than like a severe archmage whose mind has weaponized a kingdom.
Chapter 12 finally puts him bodily on page: grey-haired, gaunt, scholar-thin, pale-eyed, and robed in darkened cloth that still preserves the bones of Kirin Tor cut beneath it.

He carries himself like a senior lecturer in a room built for work rather than spectacle: hands folded on the desk, chalk easy in his grip, posture so controlled the body itself becomes part of the argument.

Chapter 13 shows him fully in the room Sylvanas sees: gaunt, pale-eyed, rising from behind the desk without hurry, then folding back behind it with blood dark down the robe after the throat shot lands.

voiceMarkers.detail: No direct dialogue on page in chapter 8. He is characterized instead through systems that speak in his style: precision, layered design, delegated doctrine, and contempt for anything less than full control.

Chapter 12 makes the voice plain, calm, and academically intimate. He speaks in theorem language, answers moral accusation without raising his voice, and lets approval show as dangerous warmth whenever Jaina reaches the next correct step.
The key register is clipped certainty rather than ranting villainy: 'Good. Then begin there.' 'Yes.' 'At last.' Even his arrogance arrives as pedagogical patience.
Chapter 13 keeps the voice calm to the end. He greets Sylvanas like a late colleague, praises Jaina without spite, and uses his last breath on one more useful line of data instead of plea or curse.

beliefs.belief: Forbidden knowledge is worth using, not merely understanding, if it can produce durable results.
beliefs.trueOrFalse: Implied by chapter 8 through the plague architecture and supported by canon material on his fall from the Kirin Tor.

beliefs.belief: Existing civic and commercial systems are best destroyed by commandeering them instead of replacing them.
beliefs.trueOrFalse: Confirmed in chapter 8 by Andorhal functioning as the ordinary throat of the rollout.

beliefs.belief: The right mind can be recruited or studied more effectively through controlled intellectual access than through blunt capture.
beliefs.trueOrFalse: Strongly implied in chapter 10 by the curated tour and the decision to let Jaina descend rather than throw her out.

beliefs.belief: A theorem does not become false because society cannot bear its use; prohibition marks cost, not refutation.
beliefs.trueOrFalse: Confirmed directly in chapter 12 by the way he frames Dalaran's ban on necromancy.

beliefs.belief: The plague is logistics and preparation for a larger war, not merely a local experiment.
beliefs.trueOrFalse: Confirmed in chapter 12 when he names the Lich King and the Burning Legion as the chain above his work.

beliefs.belief: Relation and attachment, not intelligence alone, are what finally govern whether a mind becomes his mirror or his enemy.
beliefs.trueOrFalse: Confirmed in chapter 12 when he identifies Sylvanas as the friction Jaina's own mind did not produce.

beliefs.belief: High-fidelity preserved selfhood is a solvable technical problem worth pursuing for himself.
beliefs.trueOrFalse: Confirmed in chapter 12 by the personal recurrence model he shows Jaina.

beliefs.belief: His work is larger than his present body, so even death can be met as a priced cost inside continuity.
beliefs.trueOrFalse: Supported by his calm final line and lack of panic in chapter 13.

beliefs.belief: Death is a transition rather than an ending for the mind properly prepared to survive it.
beliefs.trueOrFalse: Confirmed again in chapter 14, where Jaina carries the claim out of the sanctum and briefs it as live strategic truth.

goalsHistory.active: Continue the plague rollout north of Andorhal while keeping his location, doctrine, and higher structure partly obscured.
goalsHistory.underlying: Prove the superiority of his necromantic method over the caution of the Kirin Tor that cast him out.

goalsHistory.active: Finish the lesson, leave Jaina with the full mechanism and the scale above the plague, and hold the room steady as the next disturbance arrives.
goalsHistory.underlying: Be understood by the one surviving Kirin Tor mind he considers worthy, and prove that the difference between them is relation rather than intellect.

goalsAtChapterEnd.active: None. He dies in the sanctum after finishing the lesson and offering one last directional fact.
goalsAtChapterEnd.underlying: His chapter-end presence is the residue of a mind that expected the work to continue past the body.

emotionalStateHistory.state: Off-page and unreadable in human terms, but the machine he built suggests cold assurance: the city reacts as if compromise was anticipated and budgeted for in advance.

Composed, intellectually pleased, and almost serenely candid. Jaina's understanding gratifies him more than her agreement would have, and the ward disturbance at the end registers first as amused curiosity rather than alarm.

emotionalStateAtChapterEnd.state: Dead. The final affect is composed irritation more than fear, as if interruption offends him more than the end itself.

decisionsThisChapter.detail: Keeps the architect himself off-page while his network continues operating through city cells, repack routines, and delegated necromantic enforcement.
Maintains a doctrine that spends compromised operatives rather than risking a live breach of the command layer.
Pushes distribution beyond Andorhal before the investigators can fully classify the city, leaving the protagonists with proof that arrives after deployment has already widened.
Allows Jandice to serve as the curated face of Scholomance, turning a security problem into a recruitment-style demonstration.
Grants Jaina deeper access rather than forcing a crude confrontation, proving he prefers asymmetrical control to visible denial.
Teaches Jaina the completed resonance model directly instead of withholding it, trusting the structure of the room and the beauty of the theorem to do more work than coercion would.
Answers the ethical indictment head-on, separating theoretical soundness from civic tolerability rather than pretending the moral question is irrelevant.
Names the Lich King and the Burning Legion as the chain above his work, widening the room from private lesson to strategic briefing.
Shows Jaina the deliberate recurrence model he intends for himself, making his own death part of the lesson rather than a hidden contingency.
Greets Sylvanas without visible alarm and lets his honest regard for Jaina harden the room instead of softening it.
Tries to break for the far door and then to turn the room with a floor-borne pulse once combat begins.
Uses his last breath on the northeast corridor warning rather than any self-preserving plea.

abilitiesDemonstrated.ability: Designs plague systems that marry arcane theory, food distribution, civic routine, and covert organization into one durable weapon.
Builds subordinates and doctrine capable of surgical remote witness silencing inside a living city.
Embeds command secrecy inside ordinary commerce so effectively that investigators can map the machine and still be behind it.
Builds anti-Dalaran academic space out of recognizably Kirin Tor methods, making the institution legible and seductive to the exact minds most likely to oppose it.
Uses ward architecture and delegated authority to track visitors from the outer boundary inward without appearing on the floor himself.
Can teach complex necromantic theory in a register that feels indistinguishable from elite Kirin Tor seminar work until the human cost catches up with the content.
Maintains control of a room by stillness, precision, and timing rather than spectacle, turning calmness itself into pressure.
Reads building-scale ward responses quickly enough to notice an incoming descent before Jaina fully processes it.
Can turn a scholar's room into live combat geometry in a heartbeat, pushing ward power through floor and exits instead of needing spectacle.
Keeps verbal and bodily control almost to the instant of death, making even his last line part of the information economy of the chapter.

howOthersAddressHim.speaker: Corin
howOthersAddressHim.pattern: Says the name like a hazard, more warning than citation.

howOthersAddressHim.speaker: Jaina Proudmoore
howOthersAddressHim.pattern: Uses the name as recognition and indictment at once: the scholarly mind behind the plague finally made personal.

howOthersAddressHim.speaker: Antonidas
howOthersAddressHim.pattern: Does not ornament the name; his silence before answering is the real reaction.

howOthersAddressHim.speaker: Jandice Barov
howOthersAddressHim.pattern: Refers to him as 'the master' when the private lower authority comes into view, revealing both hierarchy and habitual deference.

howOthersAddressHim.speaker: Jaina Proudmoore
howOthersAddressHim.pattern: Uses the name directly all through chapter 12, not as rumor anymore but as a present scholarly adversary she must answer in real time.

howHeAddressesOthers.pattern: Calls her 'Jaina' once the exchange settles into shared proof-work, using first-name intellectual intimacy as both compliment and trap.
Addresses her as 'Mistress Barov' at the threshold, formal enough to place her and dismiss her in the same breath.
Greets her as Ranger-General, formal enough to acknowledge rank and casual enough to deny her the satisfaction of feeling like an intruder.

bodyLanguagePatterns.detail: Not available on page. Chapter 8 characterizes him by the disciplined posture of his systems rather than by any direct bodily presence.
Chapter 12 gives him small, economical gestures: folding his hands, turning chalk between his fingers, touching one spot on the desk like a lecturer marking the true line of a problem.

The rare slips matter because they are so brief — a flash of approval when Jaina reaches the right conclusion, a single beat of impatience when she insists on the sanctity of bodies, then composure restored at once.

His physical calm is part of the pressure: rising without hurry, hands staying on the desk after the arrow lands, and collapsing with the posture of a man who never stopped thinking in seminar cadence.

knowledge.detail: Was once Kirin Tor and once sat on the Council of Six before expulsion for necromantic work.
Is operating somewhere north of Andorhal rather than inside the city itself.
Stands above the city cells as the one mind attached to the plague's intellectual quality and long planning horizon.
Knows Jaina crossed the outer ward at Caer Darrow and chose to keep reading instead of withdrawing.
Now emerges as more than a distant vector; his methods, notation, and hidden authority are physically embedded in Scholomance's daily life.
Holds the completed resonance model and explains the distinction between low-fidelity village undead and deliberate preserved selfhood as a matter of structure, cost, and purpose.
Discloses that the plague serves the Lich King in Northrend and that the Burning Legion sits above that chain as the larger invasion frame.
Treats his own future death as priced into the system; the personal recurrence design is already a solved research question to him.
Chapter 13 confirms he knows the sanctum's geometry and the northeastern route well enough to identify its strongest line while dying.
By chapter 14 the road party treats Kel'Thuzad's expected lich return as field intelligence rather than as one private horror from the sanctum.

Chapter 18 confirms that killing Kel'Thuzad removed one relay, not the whole design. His network logic survives him strongly enough that Jaina now models the plague as a continuing broadcast problem rather than a dead man's closed experiment.

arcHistory.currentPosition: Chapter 8 introduces Kel'Thuzad in absence: named at last as the human architect whose scholarly rigor, Kirin Tor history, and willingness to use any method make him Jaina's dark mirror.

Chapter 12 completes the reveal. Kel'Thuzad is no longer institutional presence alone but fully embodied dark mirror: calm, lucid, pedagogical, and more frightening for sounding like a teacher whose methods actually work.

Chapter 12 lets Kel'Thuzad make his own case out loud. He remains absolutely committed to the lie that theoretical success outranks moral prohibition, but the chapter sharpens the real engine beneath it: scholarship, continuity, and the conviction that attachment is the only thing that kept Jaina from becoming him.

arc.lie: Knowledge justifies itself if the method works strongly enough.
arc.truth: Not accepted by the character on page; chapter 8 only establishes the cost of his opposite creed.
arc.currentPosition: Chapter 18 keeps Kel'Thuzad dead but makes his absence even more active. He is now the proof that the system was never one man deep: remove the architect, and the pattern still answers.
